mission: To make everyday underwear for Indian women that stays breathable through heat, humidity, discharge, water-washing, and long days — without polyester, nylon, spandex, or chemically processed fabrics.
vision: To move Indian intimatewear away from synthetic secrecy and toward garments that understand the body, the climate, and the rituals of daily feminine life.
positioning: Niraé sits between lingerie and hygiene: not period-tech, not performance underwear, but natural daily intimates for women who want dryness without feeling sealed in.
purpose: To give Indian women a cleaner, calmer first layer — one that respects skin, weather, scent, and the quiet discomforts women are taught to tolerate.
manifesto: We are done pretending daily underwear is simple. In India, it meets sweat, discharge, water, heat, blood, commute, office chairs, scooter rides, and a hundred private adjustments no one talks about.

Niraé begins where most intimatewear brands look away: the dampness after washing with water, the smell trapped by synthetics, the strange plastic confidence of disposable period products. We believe the answer is not more coating, more perfume, or more engineered slickness.

Our language is softness with a spine. Burgundy, butter cream, blush, powder blue, antique gold — a boudoir palette for a practical revolution, because the most intimate thing a woman owns should feel considered, not clinical.
identityIntro: Niraé’s identity is built like an heirloom vanity object with a radical material ethic inside. The burgundy script wordmark carries warmth and femininity; the serif caps bring authority; the rounded lowercase serif keeps the brand tender and breathable.

The system borrows from vintage Indian gift packaging, lace slips, apothecary labels, and monsoon-washed mornings. Antique gold is used sparingly, like a clasp or seal, while ivory and butter cream give the brand air — never hospital white, never synthetic neon.

world.title: Monsoon
world.body: Niraé speaks to the climate Indian bodies actually live in: bathroom water, sticky afternoons, damp fabric after a commute, and the slow discomfort of trapped heat. The world should feel dewy but never wet, using powder blue and ivory to signal breathability rather than sterile freshness.

world.title: Boudoir
world.body: The visual mood is vintage feminine without becoming fragile: lace edges, floral shadows, scalloped labels, and burgundy script that feels handwritten on a dressing table note. This is intimacy with taste, not lingerie theatre — sensual because it is honest.

world.title: Botanical
world.body: The brand’s material stance is central: no polyester, no nylon, no spandex, no chemically processed fabrics against the vulva. Natural fibres should be photographed close enough to see weave, grain, and softness, making the absence of plastic feel desirable rather than medicinal.

world.title: Ritual
world.body: Niraé honours the small rituals women rarely name: rinsing with water, changing before sleep, folding underwear into a drawer, carrying a spare on humid days. Packaging should open like a personal keepsake, because daily wear deserves the ceremony usually reserved for occasion lingerie.

statement: For too long, intimatewear has treated Indian women as if they live in air-conditioned brochures. Niraé is designed for the real weather of the body — heat, dampness, discharge, water, odour, and the need to feel clean without being wrapped in synthetics.

Our stance is simple and strict: the first layer should not feel like plastic, smell like chemistry, or trap the very moisture it claims to solve. Beauty is not decoration here; it is the way we make a practical truth easier to choose every day.

beliefs.title: Dry should not mean sealed
beliefs.body: The category often sells dryness through synthetic barriers, coatings, and performance language borrowed from sportswear. Niraé defines dryness differently: as airflow, absorption, and a fabric relationship that lets the body remain a body.

beliefs.title: The vulva deserves material honesty
beliefs.body: Underwear is not a seasonal trend piece; it is in constant contact with the most sensitive skin on the body. That is why our material choices must be legible, strict, and proudly stated on pack, not hidden in fine print.

beliefs.title: Femininity can be practical and ornate
beliefs.body: A product built for discharge, odour, and humidity does not need to look clinical to be credible. Niraé uses burgundy, blush, butter cream, antique gold, and delicate typographic detail to insist that utility can still feel cherished.

quotes: No plastic confidence. No damp resignation.
Made for Indian weather, and the weather of being a woman.

concept: A soft-script wordmark with a deliberate exhale in the letterforms: rounded, unhurried, never lingerie-sharp.
Pair it with a small crest device — a cotton bloom held like a secret — to make daily underwear feel ceremonial, not clinical.
The intent is to move away from period-tech sterility and into breathable Indian intimacy: ornate, gentle, and quietly radical.
logo.colorNote: The primary logo lives in burgundy on ivory or butter cream; antique gold is reserved for crest details, seals, and packaging moments where the brand should feel gifted rather than sold.

variations.name: script wordmark
variations.use: Primary expression for packaging fronts, website headers, garment labels, and any moment where softness and recognition matter first.

variations.name: crest seal
variations.use: Use on waistband tabs, tissue paper, stickers, care cards, and closures; it should feel like a promise of cotton against skin.

variations.name: stacked lockup
variations.use: For narrow packs, social avatars, marketplace tiles, and retail signage where the wordmark needs support from the crest.

variations.name: plain serif caps
variations.use: For ingredient, fabric, size, and hygiene information; a calm authority layer that prevents the brand from becoming overly decorative.

safety: Keep clear space equal to the height of the wordmark’s lowercase body around all sides.
Use the crest no smaller than 14mm in print or 48px digitally so the cotton-bloom detail remains legible.
On patterned lace or florals, place the logo inside an ivory cartouche rather than letting it fight the background.
donts: Never render the logo in black; deep navy is the darkest permitted tone.
Never add synthetic shine, chrome effects, or plasticky gradients.
Never stretch the script to look more fashion or compress it to fit a layout.
Never place burgundy directly on deep navy; the intimacy becomes heavy and bruised.
Never use the crest as a busy wallpaper at small scale.
Never pair the mark with hard-edged tech icons or medical crosses.

philosophy: The palette is built like an heirloom underwear drawer in an Indian summer: burgundy for body warmth, ivory and butter for breath, pastels for relief, and antique gold for the small ritual of being cared for. It rejects the cold whites and aqua-clean codes of hygiene brands; this is freshness without laboratory distance.

Every colour must feel absorbent, tactile, and skin-adjacent rather than synthetic. Burgundy gives the brand adult confidence, powder blue brings monsoon coolness, blush and lilac soften the conversation around discharge and wetness, while deep navy grounds the system with seriousness when discussing fabric truth and no-chemical claims.

hierarchy.title: STRUCTURE
hierarchy.sub: IVORY AIR / DEEP MONSOON NAVY / BURGUNDY BLOOM
hierarchy.body: Use Ivory Air as the dominant surface, Deep Monsoon Navy for information, and Burgundy Bloom for ownership. This keeps the system breathable first, then credible, then emotionally memorable.

hierarchy.title: ABUNDANCE
hierarchy.sub: BUTTER LINING / BLUSH PINK / LILAC / ANTIQUE MARIGOLD GOLD
hierarchy.body: Layer Butter Lining with blush and lilac in florals, lace borders, tissue, inserts, and limited drops to create the boudoir-gift world. Antique Marigold Gold should appear as a thread of detail — a rim, seal, or tiny flourish — never as a flat luxury flood.

hierarchy.title: HIGHLIGHT
hierarchy.sub: POWDER RINSE / BURGUNDY BLOOM / IVORY AIR
hierarchy.body: Use Powder Rinse to signal cooling, daily dryness, breathable comfort, and Indian-weather relief. Pair it with Burgundy Bloom for contrast so the brand does not drift into baby-care softness or generic wellness pastels.

philosophy: The typography should feel like a keepsake found in a grandmother’s dressing table drawer, then re-cut for a modern Indian bathroom shelf. It borrows from vintage boudoir labels and gilt gift packaging, but refuses fragility: this is softness with a spine.

Headlines carry the romance; body copy carries the science. The system should make cotton, breathability, discharge, wetness, odour, and daily underwear feel speakable without becoming clinical or coy.

fonts.name: Boudoir Serif
fonts.role: Headlines, product names, editorial claims, ritual language
fonts.note: Cormorant Garamond gives the brand its antique-lace intelligence: high-contrast, feminine, and literary without becoming bridal or cosmetic.

fonts.name: Bathroom Sans
fonts.role: Body copy, care instructions, social overlays, fit notes, ingredient explanations
fonts.note: Nunito Sans brings warm rounded clarity, making intimate explanations feel human, washable, and easy to trust.

scale.name: H1
scale.use: For campaign headlines, launch statements, and large packaging moments where the brand should feel sensorial and unforgettable.

scale.name: H2
scale.use: For product names, collection titles, website section headers, and educational chapter openers.

scale.name: H3
scale.use: For small authority labels such as FABRIC, WEATHER, DAILY WEAR, NO SYNTHETICS, and CARE.

scale.name: Body
scale.use: For fit notes, fabric explanations, FAQ answers, and any copy that must make intimate discomfort feel normal to discuss.

rules: Set headline serif in burgundy #8A1735 or deep navy #071923; never in pale pastels on ivory.
Use uppercase serif sparingly for heirloom cues: collection names, seals, and limited-edition packaging only.
Use the sans for all functional truth: absorbency explanations, washing, sizing, fabric composition, and warnings.
Social overlays may use bold Nunito Sans in deep navy, but pair with one small serif flourish to avoid looking like activewear.
Avoid faux-handwritten fonts for body copy; script belongs only in logo-like moments, not information.
Keep tracking generous on small caps and tight on large serif headlines, creating the feeling of pressed stationery rather than wellness packaging.

inUse.title: not plastic. not damp. not dramatic.
inUse.body: Set in Boudoir Serif, this line owns the category tension directly: the woman is not the problem, the material is. The rhythm should feel like a whispered verdict, with each phrase given its own line on butter cream.

inUse.title: made for indian weather
inUse.body: Use Bathroom Sans in uppercase with wide tracking, almost like a garment stamp. It should sit near fabric close-ups, sweat-shadow photography, or drying-line visuals so the claim feels lived, not decorative.

inUse.title: cotton where it counts
inUse.body: Set the first word in serif and the rest in sans to balance sensuality with certainty. This line belongs on inner flaps, gusset diagrams, and comparison cards against polyester-heavy period products.

inUse.title: the everyday panty that does not trap the day
inUse.body: Use body copy to explain moisture, mucus, water-washing, odour, and airflow without euphemism. The tone should be calm and exact, like an older sister who actually knows the climate.

intentIntro: Niraé must feel unlike the current intimatewear aisle: less sporty, less teenage, less synthetic-clean, less ashamed of biology. The brand should carry the emotional texture of a vintage vanity box, but speak with the clarity of a founder who created this because the existing options felt unbearable.

Every design choice should hold the tension between softness and refusal. Burgundy gives the brand its blood-warm authority; butter cream and ivory create skin-adjacent calm; blush, powder blue, lilac, and antique gold add the romance of a private object worth keeping.

patternStory.title: the monsoon lace grid
patternStory.body: The pattern system turns lace into climate logic: delicate florals, tiny droplets, airy mesh dots, and rabbit-ear curves arranged like a breathable jaali. It should feel ornate from afar and practical up close, suggesting ventilation, softness, and the small private rituals of changing, washing, drying, and starting again. Use burgundy linework over ivory for everyday pieces, antique gold over blush for gifting, and powder blue accents when speaking about freshness or water.

heroStory.title: the cotton rabbit
heroStory.body: A rabbit genuinely belongs here: not as a cute mascot, but as a symbol of alertness, softness, and bodily instinct. Draw it like an antique crest animal with long ears forming a subtle droplet or gusset shape, sitting inside a lace oval rather than a cartoon circle. It becomes the mark for comfort tested against Indian heat: quick to notice discomfort, soft enough to stay close.

patternSystem: Scale: use micro lace at 6–10mm repeats for gusset papers, tags, and inner packaging; use oversized florals at 80–140mm for campaign backdrops.
Opacity: keep patterns at 12–18% on ivory for education pages, 35–45% for gift packaging, and 100% only for borders or crest details.
Cropping: crop florals boldly off the edge, as if the viewer is peeking into a vanity drawer; avoid centred wallpaper symmetry.
Layering: combine one lace motif, one droplet motif, and one fine-line botanical maximum; never stack every decorative cue at once.
When to avoid: do not use ornate patterns on medical explanations, size charts, or absorbency diagrams; those moments need clean navy type and generous space.

philosophy: Photography should make Indian humidity visible without making the body feel exposed. Think damp bathroom tiles, cotton drying on a balcony line, a hand rinsing fabric under a tap, morning light on ivory sheets, and the relief of changing into something dry.
The gaze is intimate but never voyeuristic. We photograph gestures, textures, edges, folds, waistbands, water beads, and lived-in spaces more often than full bodies.
Colour behaves like memory: burgundy shadows, butter cream light, blush skin-warmth, powder blue water, antique gold hardware, and deep navy for night or monsoon contrast.

inUse.title: FRAMING
inUse.body: Crop into waistbands, gussets, seams, cotton grain, and the hand-touch of the garment. The image should answer one question at a time: how soft, how breathable, how dry, how un-plastic.

inUse.title: LIGHT
inUse.body: Use diffused window light, tiled reflections, and soft shadows that suggest real Indian homes. Avoid icy clinical lighting; the brand is solving discomfort, not selling a hospital textile.

inUse.title: CASTING
inUse.body: Show Indian women across skin tones, ages, and body shapes with ease rather than empowerment theatre. Postures should be private and unperformed: sitting at the bed edge, folding laundry, reaching for a fresh pair after a shower.

inUse.title: TEXTURE
inUse.body: Every shoot needs tactile proof: cotton pilling slightly, a water droplet on tile, lace shadow on packaging, fabric moving on a drying line. These details make the no-polyester position feel materially true.

intent: The logo should evolve as a refined script wordmark with a small crest-like monogram for seals, gusset stamps, tissue paper, and care cards. Pair ornate vintage script with classic serif caps for titles, a soft rounded lowercase serif for explanatory copy, and a bold geometric sans only for social overlays, callouts, and ingredient-style material claims.

Against the category, Niraé should not sound like lingerie, femtech, or wellness hygiene. It should own a more specific territory: climate-intelligent intimatewear, naturally made, sensorially beautiful, and frank about the damp, inconvenient realities women have been managing in silence.
